sentences.text: Yeah, well, that was already during the bombardment and after my father had left.
Up to that time, we were still in our apartment.
And I want to mention one thing quick.
There was some gas left in the pipes, high, because we were high up.
So we could still warm up some water.
And there were hundreds of people in this apartment building in the lower and the entrance [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] doorways and in the halls on the bottom.
And they were all people, not so much refugees then, the people who-- whose houses already had burned down or had collapsed.
So they were there, and many with small children.
And my mother was extremely busy making tea and warming some soup and, and always bringing food or bringing it down to these people.
And I never forget how people were blessing my mother.
I mean, they were all, you know, Polish woman.
And they would always say that God is going to be good to her because they were-- they were so grateful for what she was doing.
So and my mother always remembered that.
And sometimes, you know, after the war and we all survived-- my, my parents survived too-- so that maybe, maybe that helped, all the blessings that she got from helping people.
I also, I remember one funny incident where we were sitting in this kind of shelter on some steps, and I was scratching.
And I had long braids, very dense hair, long braids, and I was scratching my head.
And I was sitting next to my mother, and she said, gee, you scratch as if you had lice.
And I said, well, maybe I have lice.
And so she looked, and yeah, there were some lice in my hair from all these people there.
And my mother, oh, my daughter has lice.
My daughter has lice.
And she dragged me upstairs and washed my hair.
I think they put some turpentine on it or something and wrapped it up.
But she cut-- I had these braids that were going to below my waist, and they were my pride and joy.
And she just cut them off and made my hair very short and put that turpentine or whatever.
It stank terribly.
But she cured me.
[LAUGHS]
So anyway, that was also after my father had left.
But as I mentioned, she was bringing the stuff into the bathroom and arranging it.
And we had a little dog, a pinscher.
It was a little black dog.
Maybe weighed about, I don't know, seven, seven, eight pounds short hair.
And the dog was always with us.
And I was going up and down bringing stuff down.
And at one point-- I don't remember when it was.
Must have been maybe September 15 or there about.
I don't remember.
The-- the dog, who was running with me, wouldn't let me go past the third floor.
He was pulling on my anklets and on my shoes and barking.
And I looked at all the planes that were flying around and the bombing because we were already so kind of immune to it.
So this house, as I mentioned it was a very modern house with tremendous glass windows.
So I could see the windows were broken.
But I could see all these planes.
And I got the funny feeling.
And I went back to the-- to the bathroom where my mother was.
And she asked me why I came back.
I said, you know, Tootie, that's the name of the dog, didn't want to go anymore.
And I had a funny feeling.
So she said, well, you may be tired so I go.
And then I got embarrassed about it so I said, no, no, I go.
And I went again, and I only reached the third floor.
And the dog started the same thing-- just wouldn't let me go any higher.
And I got that funny feeling again.
So I went back.
And no sooner did I come to the bathroom when-- when two bombs fell.
And you know, there was a lot of-- of debris and-- and glass and-- and-- and a lot of dust.
It was very dark.

And my mother had thrown herself-- when the bombs hit, my mother had thrown-- had thrown herself on top of myself and the maid who was in the shelter but also had the funny feeling and came up from the shelter at about the same time that I came and asking my mother to come down in the shelter.
So we were-- both the maid and I and my mother were in this rather small bathroom, and my mother threw herself on top of us.
And-- and-- but I was injured.
And she felt something warm.
It was so dark that you-- from all the dust and stuff-- that you couldn't really see.
But she felt something warm and sticky, and she said, did anybody get hurt?
And I had a pain above my, my eye.
So she says-- so I said, I think I did-- and-- near my eye.
And my mother says, can you see?
And I said, I don't know.
And she said, what do you-- you mean you don't know?
Are you crazy?
What do you mean you don't know?
So I thought, gee, maybe I'm crazy.
And I remember I started to repeat my name.
I said, that I'm Greifinger, you know, and it was a lot and-- and my address to prove to myself that I'm not crazy that it's really-- that I'm still all here.
And she-- so she really was sure that I got hit in the head and went crazy because she heard me say my name and what not.
But anyway, then it cleared up a little bit.
And she-- she looked at it.
And at first she saw a big-- there was a bloody mass.
But what happened, I was hit where my eyebrows are, below my eyebrows, and my upper lid kind of fell and covered-- covered my eye.
So all she could see was this flesh or something.
But when she lifted the-- the eyelid, I-- you know, she saw it was OK, and she bandaged it and so on.
So-- but the dog saved my life because when the bombs hit, people on the sixth and fifth floor, there were a few that were killed.
And on the fourth, they were badly injured.
And just, you know, there weren't many people.
Most of them were in the-- in the shelter.
But the few that were there.
And I would have been upstairs on the fifth floor and probably gotten-- gotten killed.
I was 15.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I really feel that the dog had saved my life then.
After that, after the Germans occupied Warsaw, I-- we moved to my father's office.
We were not touched.
We were in a different area of Warsaw.
And people who worked for this-- for Standard Oil and who didn't have where to stay also moved in.
And we bedded down on some desks.
And we slept on the desks, and there was some soup kitchen established so we were getting one warm meal in the soup kitchen.
And here again was a funny.
One time, we were going in to a soup, and a friend of mine who just came out, she said, the soup is wonderful today.
There's meat in it, but, you know, it's horse meat.
So I just shrugged my shoulders and what.
And-- and I told my mother.
And she said-- I said, you know, it's horse meat.
And she, oh, come on now, and, you know, big joke.
And then we-- she ate the soup, and she enjoyed it.
And then on the way out, we met this other woman who my mother know-- knows, and she worked in that soup kitchen.
And this woman said, wasn't the meal good today?
My mother said, yes, was wonderful.
And she said, you know, it's just the first time we used horse meat.
My mother just start to throw up.
I thought she's going to faint on the street.
And, you know, she-- awful.
And I just bring it up because later on we would do anything for a piece of horse meat.
You know, but there were so many horses that were killed in the street, and people would just come and cut them up and then just take parts of it.
It was just hard.
There-- hundreds and hundreds of people had been killed in these-- in these basements and-- and the shelters because the houses just collapsed.
Oh, after I was injured, after our house was hit, we moved to a sub-basement.
And it was, like, two stories below the first floor.
And there was all the coal that was used for heating purposes and heating the water.
And I was-- my mother bedded me down on a pile of coal.
And it was September, and it was a hot-- hot summer.
And there was no air to breathe.
There was a lot of coal dust.
And it was just horrid.
And I had very high temperature from my injury.
And the-- the-- the janitor also stayed in the sub-basement.
Now, this janitor, we always had, I thought, a good relationship with.
I know that my parents were always generous, and, you know, every month they would give him a nice monthly gift, Christmas.
And he was always very polite and very nice.
But-- and he knew where there was some water, which pipes still had some water.
And so he took some water, put a basin down, and let his kids-- he had two children-- play in the water, you know, because it was so hot.
And I begged for a little bit of water.
And he wouldn't give it to me.
And he said, that-- that he's not going to give any water to the Jew.
And I-- I still have these nightmares, you know, because I--I was, I guess, a little delirious too.
I was very high temperature.
And he wouldn't-- and he put that basin not far from where I was lying.
And he put it so that-- the-- the kids were splashing.
It was like two or three meters from me that the water drops are falling.
And my mother was afraid to start a fight with him because, you know, he was the--the man there, and my mother was afraid.
But luckily, I-- I wasn't-- the next day or so she got some water.
That was during the night when I was burning up with fever.
So that was a terrible shock to me because I just could not understand why all of a sudden, this man that we always thought were-- we had a good relationship with all of a sudden got so nasty.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Well, so first we'd stayed on-- on my father-- in my father's office on-- on the desks there and ate in the soup kitchen.
And some of the food that my mother had canned-- it wasn't cans.
It was in glass jars-- survived.
They were in the bath tub.
And also I was injured, but some of these glass jars survived, and so we-- we took them out of my father's office.
And then my mother was exchanging that for some bread and some other stuff because the soup that we were getting really wasn't enough.
After that, we moved to an apartment again.
Then my father came back.

And my father had, again, he had the key of-- from an apartment in a different section in more suburban-- well, it was not a suburbs, but not center city-- of an apartment that belonged to a journalist.
And he was both a lawyer and journalist.
And he had left just before the war broke out, he left to America.
And he gave the key to my father.
And we went and investigated, and the house was standing.
The windows were broken.
And so we moved in there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this-- what I remember of that winter, it was a very, very severe winter.
The schools were closed and not only for-- like, the high schools were all closed, and not only for Jewish kids but all the Gentile kids.
The high schools were closed, but the vocational schools were open because they-- I guess the Germans needed the-- the people who are trades people or people who could work with their hands.
So, but-- and elementary schools were open, but I understand that there was no-- they didn't teach geography or history in the elementary school.
That was it.
And also in the beginning, they immediately arrested the Polish intelligentsia.
And again, that was Jews and non-Jews alike-- I mean, teachers, lawyers, doctors, priests, anybody who could be in a leadership position and organize some resistance.
And there were-- well, some people, I guess, were sent away.
But I understand that over 2,000 were shot in the environment of Warsaw in the forest.
I forgot the name of the forest.
But it was--
Yes.
Because people were talking.
I mean, we weren't in the ghetto so people were talking about it.
So that was known.
And-- because they would come to an apartment, take the people or take these men out.
And they even called this area 'a circle of death' because it was surrounded Warsaw.
And there were over 2,000 people that were shot in this in 1939.
And they were all from the higher echelon in society.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we stayed in this apartment [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- in the winter.
And I remember, it was-- it was in the fall, the maid got typhoid fever.
That was like dysentery, you know, like salmonella or something.
And she had this very high fever and diarrhea.
And my mother was nursing her.
And she would not have, I guess, survived if it weren't for my mother.
I still remember my mother there constantly giving her-- you know, making cold compresses and so on.
I also remember this apartment had one room that was-- the walls were lined with books.
He was a journalist and a lawyer, and he had some books.
And the one time some Germans came in, and they [LAUGHS] didn't like the books.
They looked around, they-- they got some Jews from the street and started to throw them out the window.
The books.
And so that was, again, my encounter with German culture, you know.
We-- and then when winter came, it was extremely cold.
So the only warm room was the entrance foyer because it had four walls.
There were no windows.
And we bedded down in this entrance foyer.
There were two mattresses.
And my mother and father slept on one, and I and the maid slept on the other one.
And since there were no-- my father was still going to work because that was before America entered the wars, and that was an American company.
So he could still go to the office.
And he was still drawing a salary at that time.
But he insisted that I occupy my time in some constructive way, so I-- I-- I got somewhere a-- a book, textbook for shorthand.
And I was studying shorthand.
And I got the typewriter that this journalist had in his apartment, and I put it on-- on the floor.
I was sitting on the floor.
And I had the typewriter on the floor.
There was no room for me to-- to sit.
And I was trying to type.
I taught myself how to type.
Wasn't touch-typing, it was just with two fingers.
But shorthand and typing.
Well, again, and that-- the order was given already in, I think, was October or November of 1939 that all Jews had to wear white armband with a blue Mogen David.
And this was for means of identification.
So naturally, and-- and, you know, punishment for being caught without it.
And again, people were standing in line for bread, for instance.
And any hoodlum could come, and if you were closer to the store for bread, they could chase you out.
You, Jew, go in the end of the line.
These same people would be caught in the street for some labor.
The Germans really had a ball with the Orthodox Jews.
You know, they wore their special garbs and had the beard.
So frequently, you could see that they were pulling on their beards and making them cut-- cutting their beards.
I saw, at one point, where a German officer was standing there and made an old Jew lie down on the floor and lick his boots.
You had to get off the sidewalk when you saw a German.
And men had to take their hats off.
And sometimes when you got off the sidewalk and you looked in the German's face, you could get beaten up, you know, with the remark, how dare you look in my face.
And when you learned your lesson and the next time you went into the-- got off the sidewalk and didn't look, so, what's wrong with you?
Why don't you-- why can't you look into-- into my eyes?
And get beaten up for that.
Naturally, the bank accounts were frozen.
And Jews could only have so much money.
So even if they had some kind of a business, they weren't allowed to have more money, you know.
It just didn't-- didn't pay to-- to have a business.
You just-- and most of the businesses, anyway, were taken away.
And some German Volksdeutscher, which is ethnic German, was put in as an administrator.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it-- it was very, very rough for most people.
And luckily, as I say, my father was still working at that time.
Yes.
Yes.
Because that was before the ghetto was established.
Yes, but-- but even so, he could continue to work because he was working for an American firm?
I think he had some ID, some certificate, that he is working for this American firm.

So he, you know-- but only people who had their own businesses-- tradespeople, shoe makers, and what not-- they-- if it was a small shoemaker, he could still be a shoemaker, but he couldn't have any money.
He couldn't earn much money.
And-- and the money that he could have was not enough to-- to live on so-- And then, the rumors started about the ghetto being established.
And I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and again, it wasn't just straightforward.
There was one, you know, either it was there will be a ghetto; there won't be a ghetto.
And the ghetto will be in this part of this town.
It will be so large, and it will be smaller.
And so people were completely off guard.
They didn't know what was happening.
There was a lot of confusion.
But then they finally, in 1940, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- they had the ghetto.
They built the ghetto.
The ghetto was surrounded by a 11-foot wall, a high wall, brick wall.
And on top of that you had barbed wire.
And there were just a few gates.

And all of the Jews from Warsaw, and there were about 350,000-- one third of the population of Warsaw was Jewish before the war-- and people from all surrounding towns were put into the-- into the ghetto.
And so at one point, in, I guess in 1941, there were almost half a million people squeezed in an area that was only 2% of the area of Warsaw.
Well, we only could get one room.
And we went on Sienna Street, which was the last street near the wall.
And like-- and because part of the-- the ghetto was in a dilapidated section of Warsaw.
And only the-- the outer part was still in-- still had a few streets that were halfway decent, had halfway decent apartments.
And my parents were lucky to get a room in a big apartment.
I think it was a three, four-bedroom apartment.
And you know, and it was one of those old-fashioned apartment with a dining room and-- and a big dining room, big-- big living room.
And we got the dining room.
And I remember at that, oh, at that time, the-- the girl, Janina the maid, was not with us anymore.
She went to-- to [NON-ENGLISH], back to her-- her family.
After that point, she did not really want to leave us.
But in the ghetto-- she-- she wouldn't come into the ghetto.
So the three of us stayed in this one dining room.
And I remember my mother strung some wire across the room.

I mean, like, 2/3 of the room and put some sheets on there for privacy because people who were going into the kitchen-- and everybody had to share the kitchen-- were going through-- through that room.
And so there was constant traffic.
Every room was occupied.
So I remember this-- this wire and the sheets there.
And and-- and that's where we stayed for a while.
We-- we made some house committees where people were organizing food for people who didn't have it.
I remember a so-called 'spoon' campaign where you would go from apartment to apartment and ask for a spoon of sugar, a spoon of groats, a spoon of flour.
And you would bring that to a-- a soup kitchen.
And some apartment houses, the women themselves, they would cook and make the soup or whatever for people who were completely out of things.

Actually, the worst fate [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was I had the-- the worst off were-- were the refugees from such surrounding communities because they, to start with, most of them were poor, and they could only come in with what they could carry.
And they came a little bit later, so they couldn't get rooms and apartments so shelters were established.
And so people were in the shelters.
And in some places, they-- they couldn't even get into a shelter.
So they bedded down in basements and in attics.
And these people were the first ones to really die of hunger.
But the soup kitchens were established in-- in the ghetto.
OK, sure.
